blocks.content: Cassandra's father stood when they came in and shook Ethan's hand with the directness of a man who reads people in the first ten seconds and doesn't need longer. He was quieter than Ethan expected — not cold, but reserved in a considered way, not a default one.
Just that. Like good was the whole assessment.

Dinner was comfortable in the way of a family practiced at receiving someone new without making them the spectacle. Cassandra's mother asked about his work — genuine questions, with follow-ups — and he found himself explaining painting in a way that felt natural rather than performed.
Halfway through, her mother set down her fork.

blocks.speaker: Mother
blocks.content: She painted you a picture, you know. Did she tell you?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: She told me she made something. She said it was bad.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: She got the awning right. And there's a very expressive cloud.
Cassandra's mother laughed. Cassandra covered her face with one hand. Her father smiled at the table — the particular smile of a man who doesn't smile often but means it completely when he does.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: We are not discussing the cloud at the dinner table.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: The cloud has chaos. Ethan already told me that.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I said chaos is different from bad.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: He's an artist. Diplomacy is part of the service. He told me that too.

Her father looked at Ethan with something that might have been the beginning of approval — not the quick kind, but the kind that had been watching carefully and was satisfied with what it had seen so far.
